aliases: Oats with strawberry blueberry banana and honey

english: Oats with strawberry blueberry banana and honey

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oatmeal entry; no specific 'oatmeal with mixed fruits' entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific 'oatmeal with fruits' composite entry. A00GJ (oat-based porridge) is the closest defensible anchor. Fruit toppings are not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for oatmeal with mixed fruits composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of oatmeal with fruits is approximately 250 g, representing a typical breakfast or snack serving of boiled rolled oats topped with mixed fresh fruit and honey.

nutrient_source.title: Oatmeal with Fruits — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for 'Oatmeal with fruits' (cr_id F05050018). All per-100g macros and micronutrients reported by HPB are taken from this canonical record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked oatmeal (NDB 08121) proportionally applied to the HPB-reported total fat of 1.34 g/100g, as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry.

gi_source.title: Oatmeal, rolled oats, cooked — GI value 55 (Atkinson et al., 2008; University of Sydney GI Database)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for 'oatmeal with mixed fruits and honey' as a composite dish. GI of 55 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked rolled oats (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283). The addition of fresh fruit (strawberry, blueberry, banana) and honey will modestly raise the effective GI; however, the oat beta-glucan matrix is the dominant determinant and the composite GI is estimated to remain in the low-to-medium range (55–65). GL per serving calculated as: GI 55 × carbohydrate per serving (19.82 g/100g × 250 g serving = 49.55 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 27.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100g); moderate sugar (9.92 g/100g) with fibre 2.16 g/100g narrowly above the 2 g threshold; potassium is modest at 60 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Oatmeal with fruits is suitable for hypertension management given its negligible sodium content; however, individuals should avoid adding honey in large quantities or dried fruit toppings that would push sugar above 10 g/100g, and should favour berry-dominant fruit combinations to maximise potassium and antioxidant intake while keeping sugar controlled.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 9.92 g/100g (below 15 g gate); dietary fibre 2.16 g/100g; oat beta-glucan moderates postprandial glucose; GI approximately 55 (low) for the oat base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Oatmeal with fruits is conditionally suitable for type 2 diabetes when portion is limited to 150–200 g cooked; the oat beta-glucan content moderates glucose absorption, but the combined sugar from banana and honey warrants monitoring of postprandial glucose response, and dried fruit or additional sweeteners should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.24 g/100g), zero trans fat, soluble fibre (beta-glucan) from oats supports LDL reduction, very low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oatmeal with fruits is well-suited for cardiovascular disease prevention; the oat beta-glucan provides clinically meaningful LDL-lowering benefit at typical serving sizes, saturated fat is negligible, and the fresh fruit contributes polyphenols and antioxidants that support vascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant-rich fresh fruit (polyphenols, vitamin C from berries); whole-grain fibre supports gut immune modulation; negligible saturated fat and no trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Oatmeal with fruits is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; fresh berries provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols and vitamin C, while the oat base contributes whole-food fibre that supports gut microbiome diversity; sweetened syrups and large quantities of dried fruit should be avoided to limit pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oatmeal with Fruits is a warm, wholesome breakfast or snack dish consisting of boiled rolled oats topped with fresh strawberries, blueberries, banana slices, and a drizzle of honey. The oats are cooked by simmering in water or milk until soft and creamy, producing a porridge-like texture with a mild, slightly nutty flavour. The fresh fruit adds natural sweetness, colour, and a variety of textures — soft banana, juicy strawberry, and firm blueberry — while honey provides additional sweetness and floral aroma. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 100 kcal, 1.9 g protein, 1.34 g fat, 19.82 g carbohydrate, 9.92 g sugar, and 2.16 g dietary fibre, with very low sodium at 3 mg. The oat base is a rich source of beta-glucan, a soluble fibre with clinically demonstrated LDL-cholesterol-lowering and postprandial glucose-moderating effects. The glycemic index of the oat base is approximately 55 (low), making this dish suitable for blood sugar management when portion-controlled. It is plant-based, dairy-free, and halal-suitable. Clinically, it is appropriate for hypertension (very low sodium), cardiovascular disease prevention (beta-glucan, low saturated fat), and type 2 diabetes management with portion control. It is widely consumed in Singapore as a breakfast option and is recognised as a wholegrain, low-calorie, low-sodium food by the Health Promotion Board.
common_user_queries: Is oatmeal with fruits good for diabetes?
Calories in oatmeal with strawberry blueberry banana
Is fruit oatmeal suitable for high blood pressure?
Healthy breakfast oats with fruit Singapore
Is oatmeal with honey and fruit good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (F05050018) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and fatty acid sub-fractions estimated from USDA SR Legacy oat composition data as HPB does not separately report these; this introduces minor uncertainty for those fields only. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked rolled oats. Overall confidence is high for macros and medium for GI and fatty acid sub-fractions.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.24 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.43 g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.50 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked oatmeal (NDB 08121) proportionally applied to the HPB-reported total fat of 1.34 g/100g, as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 g/100g: naturally zero for a minimally processed plant-based oat and fresh fruit dish with no hydrogenated fats. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 mg/100g as confirmed by HPB source; naturally zero for a plant-based dish containing no animal products. 4. The following fields are set to -1 because detailed vitamin, mineral sub-fraction, and moisture data for this specific oatmeal-with-fruits composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database (the primary approved source for this record) nor available as a matched composite entry in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are set to -1 (data not available) rather than estimated, as the composite dish formulation (variable fruit ratios, honey quantity) makes component-level estimation insufficiently reliable for a canonical record. Source consulted and confirmed unavailable: HPB Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) and USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) as of 2026-04-16. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate for the oat base from the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite dish GI including banana and honey may be modestly higher (estimated 55–65 range). GL per 250 g serving = 27.2. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is consumed and catalogued in Singapore. 7. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft created from HPB Foodo source data.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types to 'closest_parent'; corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for GI 55; added -1 field justification to notes.

Iteration 3: Expanded G3 numeric hygiene documentation in notes to explicitly name every -1 field, state the reason data is unavailable (HPB Foodo does not report these for this composite dish), and cite both HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central as the approved sources consulted that confirmed unavailability. This directly addresses all 15 g3_numeric_hygiene failure reasons from the validator.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
